message.abstract:
<jats:title>Abstract</jats:title>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Importance</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Obesity and ethnicity are well characterised risk factors for severe COVID-19 outcomes, but the differential effects of obesity on COVID-19 outcomes by race/ethnicity has not been examined robustly in the general population.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Objective</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>To investigate the association between body mass index (BMI) and COVID-19 mortality across different ethnic groups.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Design, Setting, and Participants</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    This is a retrospective cohort study using linked national Census, electronic health records and mortality data for English adults aged 40 years or older who were alive at the start of pandemic (24
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    January 2020).
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Exposures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>BMI obtained from electronic health records. Self-reported ethnicity (white, black, South Asian, other) was the effect-modifying variable.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Main Outcomes and Measures</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    COVID-19 related death identified by ICD-10 codes U07.1 or U07.2 mentioned on the death certificate from 24
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    January 2020 until December 28
                    <jats:sup>th</jats:sup>
                    2020.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Results</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    The analysis included white (n = 11,074,708; mean age 61.9 [±13.4] years; 54% women), black (n = 416,542; 56.4 [±11.7] years; 57% women), South Asian (621,691; 55.7 [±12.4] years; 51% women) and other (n = 478,196; 55.3 [±11.6] years; 55% women) ethnicities with linked BMI data. The association between BMI and COVID-19 mortality was stronger in ethnic minority groups. Compared to a BMI of 22.5 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in white ethnicities, the adjusted HR for COVID-19 mortality at a BMI of 30 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in white, black, South Asian and other ethnicities was 0.95 (95% CI: 0.87-1.03), 1.72 (1.52-1.94), 2.00 (1.78-2.25) and 1.39 (1.21-1.61), respectively. The estimated risk of COVID-19 mortality at a BMI of 40 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in white ethnicities (HR = 1.73) was equivalent to the risk observed at a BMI of 30.1 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    , 27.0 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    , and 32.2 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in black, South Asian and other ethnic groups, respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Conclusions</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>This population-based study using linked Census and electronic health care records demonstrates that the risk of COVID-19 mortality associated with obesity is greater in ethnic minority groups compared to white populations.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Question</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>Does the association between BMI and COVID-19 mortality vary by ethnicity?</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Findings</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>
                    In this study of 12.6 million adults, BMI was associated with COVID-19 in all ethnicities, but with stronger associations in ethnic minority populations such that the risk of COVID-19 mortality for a BMI of 40 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in white ethnicities was observed at a BMI of 30.1 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    , 27.0 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    , and 32.2 kg/m
                    <jats:sup>2</jats:sup>
                    in black, South Asian and other ethnicities, respectively.
                  </jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
                <jats:sec>
                  <jats:title>Meaning</jats:title>
                  <jats:p>BMI is a stronger risk factor for COVID-19 mortality in ethnic minorities. Obesity management is therefore a priority in these populations.</jats:p>
                </jats:sec>
title: Obesity, Ethnicity, and Covid-19 Mortality: A population-based cohort study of 12.6 Million Adults in England
